coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of poly (4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) and its conjugated copolymers initiated by easily accessible carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Maruthapandi, Moorthy
Kumar, Vijay Bhooshan
Levine, Mindy
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: A new diamine-functionalized polyether polymer, poly-(4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) (POBBA), and copolymers containing the POBBA motif were synthesized via the use of carbon dot free radical initiators. Although previous work in our group has reported carbon dot-initiated polymerization reactions in combination with UV light, the long reaction times and requirement for UV light limited the general applicability of this method. In our present work, we report a significant improvement over our previous method by eliminating the requirement for UV light as a co-initiator and by significantly improving the reaction times. The structure of the newly synthesized POBBA and a variety of POBBA-containing copolymers were confirmed by various analytical techniques, including Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) and Solid-State NMR spectroscopy. The morphology and particle size distribution of the newly synthesized polymers were analyzed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). Kinetic studies of the polymerization reaction at varying reaction temperatures and reaction times, as well as studies on the initiator efficacy of the carbon dots, are also reported, and results highlight the facile initiation and rapid polymer generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly-(4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine)
Copolymers
C-dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Particle size measurement by dynamic light scattering (DLS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of C-dot products: (a) Optical images of the C-dot solution under ambient light and under UV-light irradiation; (b) UV–visible absorption spectrum of the C-dot solution, with two main maxima highlighted with blue arrows; (c) HRTEM images of the C-dots; and (d) Fluorescence spectra of C-dot solution with excitation at a variety of different wavelengths.
The FTIR spectra (a) (i) OBBA and (ii) POBBA, (b) copolymers of CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3.
The TGA spectra of (a) OBBA and POBBA, (b) copolymers of CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3.
The XRD spectra of POBBA and copolymers of CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3.
The SEM images of (a) POBBA and copolymers of (b) CP-1, (c) CP-2 and (d) CP-3.
DLS measurements of all newly synthesized polymers: POBBA, CP-1, CP-2, and CP-3.
Solid-state 13C NMR spectra (a) OBBA and POBBA, (b) copolymers CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3. Note that spinning side band (instrumental artifact) is marked with an asterisk.

Kinetic studies of POBBA synthesis: (a) Plot of reaction temperature vs. percent yield (measured as grams product per gram monomer); (b) Plot of the effect of initiator concentration (of C-dots) vs. reaction yield (%); and (c) Plot of the reaction time (measured in hours) vs the Conversion of monomer to polymer.

The chemical structure of POBBA and POBBA-containing co-polymers synthesized by C-dot initiated polymerization (general reaction conditions: C-dot (3 mL solution, 9 mg C-dot), acetonitrile (15 mL), nitric acid (1.5 mL), distilled water (10 mL); heating at 90 °C for 24 h).
Key peaks in the IR absorption frequency region of OBBA and POBBA and the corresponding functional groups.

row.entry: Out-of-plane bending vibration mode of NH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Key peaks in the IR absorption frequency region of CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3 and the corresponding functional groups.

row.entry: Copolymers of CP-1, CP-2, and CP-3
Out of plane bending vibration of amine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Particle sizes, D, and polydispersity indices, DI, determined by dynamic light scattering from polymer and copolymers.
Kinetic studies of reaction temperature, reaction time and initiator concentration on the isolated yields of the polymerization reaction.

row.entry: Reaction period 24 h and CDs (9 mg)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of poly (4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) and its conjugated copolymers initiated by easily accessible carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Rhode Island, 140 Flagg Road, Kingston, RI 02881, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new diamine-functionalized polyether polymer, poly-(4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) (POBBA), and copolymers containing the POBBA motif were synthesized via the use of carbon dot free radical initiators. Although previous work in our group has reported carbon dot-initiated polymerization reactions in combination with UV light, the long reaction times and requirement for UV light limited the general applicability of this method. In our present work, we report a significant improvement over our previous method by eliminating the requirement for UV light as a co-initiator and by significantly improving the reaction times. The structure of the newly synthesized POBBA and a variety of POBBA-containing copolymers were confirmed by various analytical techniques, including Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) and Solid-State NMR spectroscopy. The morphology and particle size distribution of the newly synthesized polymers were analyzed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). Kinetic studies of the polymerization reaction at varying reaction temperatures and reaction times, as well as studies on the initiator efficacy of the carbon dots, are also reported, and results highlight the facile initiation and rapid polymer generation.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials are extremely well-known materials, with significant literature reports over the last few decades focused on a number of carbon materials with diverse chemical, physical, and electrochemical properties [1,2]. Among the various types of carbon materials, nanosized carbon materials are a unique class of materials due to their small size and high levels of control over optical properties, with applications in the biomedical sciences and in the development of clean energy systems [3–7].

Within nanosized carbon materials, carbon dots (C-dots) are defined as extremely small nanomaterials (1–10 nm), and generally contain substantial quantities of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen [2,5]. C-dots have attracted considerable research interest due to their superior optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, small size, and low production costs, with high general levels of control over the product structure [8–12]. These fascinating physical and chemical properties, in turn, allow for a wide range of potential C-dot applications [4,11–14]. Several literature reports describe the rational surface functionalization of carbon dots and the ability to use such modified C-dots for targeted applications [15–17]. For example, fluorescent C-dots were used for the frontal polymerization [18], copolymer of Polycation-b-Polyzwitterion for electronic applications [19], etc.

In addition to targeted applications of surface-functionalized C-dots, C-dots can also be utilized for the fabrication of light emitting diodes or as components of polyaniline and poly (4-styrene sulfonate) based solar cells [18,20]. Moreover, Sun et al. reported that the photo-excitation of the carbon cores in C-dots results in efficient charge separation within the C-dot core, with both electron donors and acceptors formed from such excitation [21]. As a result of the charge separation, photoinduced electron transfer as a mechanism for system relaxation and charge recombination is possible. Such electron transfer was reported by Kang et al. to catalyze a series of organic transformation reactions using visible light (sunlight) as both a photoinitiator and photocatalyst [19,22]. The electron-accepting capabilities of photoexcited C-dots have been used by Zhu et al. for the C-dot promoted, free radical polymerization of water-soluble vinyl monomers and chain transfer polymerization [23,24], with the proposed role of C-dots to act as radical scavengers and maintain a low concentration of potentially interfering free radicals. As a result of such scavenging, the polymerization in the presence of C-dots yielded higher molecular weight polymers with lower observed polydispersity compared to the polymers obtained in the absence of C-dots.

Previous work by our group has reported the C-dot promoted polymerization reaction of amine-containing monomers to produce polyaniline, polypyrrole, and poly(4,4′-diamino diphenyl methane) [25,27]. and while the reaction rates and product yields were enhanced in the presence of C-dots, the week-long reaction times and the requirement for UV light detracted from the overall benefits of these polymerization conditions. The proposed reaction mechanism involved the formation of C-dot based radicals via UV light irradiation, which in turn initiated the radical polymerization.

While our previously reported C-dot promoted polymerization had numerous advantages, the requirement for UV light and the long reaction times were less than ideal. In this current work, we significantly improve on our previously reported work by reporting a relatively rapid, UV-light free one-step polymerization reaction to access poly-(4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) (POBBA) and POBBA-containing copolymers with aniline, pyrrole, and p-phenylenediamine as comonomer units. The previous use of UV light to promote free radical generation was replaced by the use of thermal heating to accomplish a similar goal, leading to effective C-dot activation. Kinetic studies of the polymerization reaction under various reaction times, reaction temperatures, and initiator concentrations are also reported, with the ideal polymerization time (as measured by polymer yield, molecular weight, and polydispersity) identified as 24 h.
PEG-400 (99.998%), 4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine, pyrrole, aniline, p-phenylenediamine, acetonitrile, and nitric acid were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Israel.

The synthesis of C-dots was performed using a sonochemical technique with a PEG-400 as described in a previous report [25]. In brief, 60 mL of PEG-400 was transferred into a 100 mL beaker and the beaker was incubated in an oil bath at 80 °C. The tip of an ultrasonic transducer was dipped in the PEG-400 and sonicated for 2 h with 60% amplitude of the sonication.

1.2 g of 4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine (OBBA) (6.2 mmol) in a 100 mL single-neck round-bottomed flask was combined with 1.5 mL of nitric acid, after which time the color of the monomer changed from a white solid to a purple solution. To this reaction mixture, 15 mL of acetonitrile, 3 mL of the prepared C-dot solution (vide supra) and 10 mL of doubly distilled water were added. The reaction mixture was mixed well and refluxed in an oil bath for 24 h at 90 ○C under ambient environment. After 24 h, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and poured in to a beaker filled with ice. The dark brown solids that precipitated were collected by filtration, washed several times with distilled water and dried at room temperature.
All copolymers were synthesized using the general procedure listed above, with a 1:1 M ratio of the two monomers used.
4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine (0.6 g, 3 mmol), C-dot solution (3 mL, 9 mg), acetonitrile (15 mL), nitric acid (1.5 mL), pyrrole (0.6 g, 10 mmol) and distilled water (10 mL).
4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine (0.64 g, 3.2 mmol), C-dot solution (3 mL, 9 mg), acetonitrile (15 mL), nitric acid (1.5 mL), aniline (0.61 g, 6.5 mmol) and distilled water (10 mL).
Poly (4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) (0.61 g, 3.2 mmol), C-dot (3 mL, 9 mg), acetonitrile (15 mL), nitric acid (1.5 mL), p-PDA (0.61 g, 6.5 mmol) and distilled water (10 mL).

All newly synthesized polymers and copolymers were characterized by several analytical techniques. FTIR spectra were obtained using a Transon 27 instrument, from Bruker Inc. (Germany), equipped with a diamond tip. The morphology of the polymers was observed using high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) with an FEI Megallon 400 L microscope. The samples for HRSEM were prepared by placing a small amount of the dried polymer powder on a carbon tape attached to a copper plate, followed by sputtering a thin gold film coating on top of the powder (Working distance is 6–7.1 mm and accelerating voltage is 5 kV). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of the polymer and copolymers were performed on ZetaSizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK). The samples were prepared by dispersing 2 mg of polymer or copolymers in 10 mL of water and bath sonicated for 10 min at 25 °C. The solid-state properties of the amorphous synthesized materials were recorded using a Bruker Inc. (Germany) AXS D8 Advance diffractometer with a reflection of θ-geometry, 40 kV and 30 mA using Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) as the radiation source, with receiving slits of 0.2 mm and a high-resolution energy-dispersive detector. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Cary Vin spectrophotometer. All MAS NMR experiments were carried out on a Bruker Advance III 5000 MHz narrow-bore spectrometer, using a 4 mm double-resonance MAS probe. 13C CPMAS were carried out at a spinning rate of 8 kHz, using a 2.5 µs 1H 90° pulse, 2 ms mixing time and a 3 s recycle delay between acquisitions. NMR spectra were acquired using a Bruker 5000 Ultra Shield spectrometer with 400 MHz NMR (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). The thermal behavior of the polymers was measured using a model Pyris 1 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), produced by Perkin Elmer. The analysis used dried samples of approximately 8 mg in a dynamic nitrogen atmosphere of 100 mL/min with a temperature range of 30–900 °C, and a heating rate of 10 °C/min. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis was carried out on a JEOL 2100 microscope, which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.
The chemical structures of all newly synthesized polymers are shown in Scheme 1 .

The reported C-dot preparation uses PEG-400 as a carbon source, with water-soluble C-dots produced via sonication-induced pyrolysis [26]. This sonication method leads to the residual presence of surface active oxygen moieties- on the C-dot products, which have been found from the use of other C-dot synthetic methods. These surface-active functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, aldehyde, and oxygen radical groups, can positively impact on the quantum yield, fluorescence properties, surface charge density, and water solubility of the C-dot products. The synthetic procedure will not be repeated here because of redundancy.

Fig. 1 a shows images of a C-dot solution taken under ambient light and under irradiation with a hand-held, long-wave TLC lamp (365 nm excitation), highlighting the strong, blue fluorescence of the C-dot solution from such excitation. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the C-dots are shown in Fig. 1b. The strong absorption peaks of C-dots at 246 nm and 360 nm can be assigned to the p–p* electron transition and graphitic nature of the carbon material, respectively. A magnified HRTEM image (Fig. 1c, insert) of individual C-dots reveals that the lattice plane of C-dots is 0.21 nm and matches with the graphite facet (1 0 0), consistent with previous literature reports of C-dot microscopic images [26]. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths (330, 350, 370, 390, 410, 430, 450, and 470 nm) and maximum emission from such excitation was observed in the range of 420–600 nm. Fig. 1d shows the typical fluorescence spectra of C-dots which obtained from PEG-400 after the sonication treatment. Meanwhile, the strongest fluorescence emission was observed with 390 nm excitation, with an emission maximum at 460 nm and a relative quantum yield of 16%, in agreement with our previously published results [26].

The FT-IR spectra of the OBBA monomer and POBBA product are shown in Fig. 2 a. The OBBA monomer has an FTIR spectrum with narrow peaks at 3445, 3364, 3322, 1619, 1485, and 1210 cm−1, which can be assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of the NH functional group (greater than 3000 cm−1), to CC aromatic stretching vibrations (between 1400 and 1700 cm−1), and to a CO stretching vibration (at 1210 cm−1). The appearance of a broad peak around 3500–3000 cm−1 in the POBBA FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2a) indicates that polymerization has occurred, in accordance with literature precedent that shows analogous peak broadening in polymer FTIR spectra [28,29], with the broad band at 3354 cm−1 assigned to NH stretching, the peak at 2901 cm−1 assigned to CH aromatic stretching, the peaks at 1604 and 1509 cm−1 assigned to CC stretching, the peak at 1318 cm−1 assigned to CN stretching, and the peak at 824 cm−1 assigned to the out-of-plane bending vibration mode of NH. A list of all relevant IR peaks and the corresponding structural information that corresponds to those peaks is given in Table 1 .

The FTIR spectra of copolymers are shown in Fig. 1b, with the spectra of copolymers CP-1, CP-2, and CP-3 showing broad bands due to a high degree of conjugation in the copolymer structure, analogous to FTIR spectra of analogous compounds [30]. The copolymers’ spectra display a broad peak at 3351 cm−1 which is assigned to the NH stretching vibration; an aromatic CCH stretching vibration at 3074 cm−1; peaks at 1605 and 1501 cm−1, which are attributed to the aromatic CC stretching vibration; peaks around 1340 cm−1 which are due to the CN stretching vibration; a peak at 1246 cm−1, which is due to the ether CO stretching vibration; and a peak at 830 cm−1, which is attributed to out of plane bending vibration of an amine group. The characteristic peaks and peak broadening of in the FTIR spectra indicate the successful polymerization of all monomers. A summary of all relevant IR peaks and the corresponding structural information is shown in Table 2 .

The thermal stability of OBBA, POBBA and copolymers CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3 were analyzed using thermogravimetric analysis. Fig. 3 a shows the direct comparison of the OBBA monomer with the POBBA polymer, and indicates markedly higher thermal stability of POBBA compared to its constituent monomer, which loses 91% of its mass at 298 °C. The TGA weight loss of POBBA, by contrast, displayed three distinct weight loss stages (marked by dashed lines on the spectra): (1) Minor weight loss (4%) at 97–135 °C, attributed to the evaporation of water; (2) 12% weight loss between 468 and 664 °C, due to thermal decomposition of oligomers [31,32]; and (3) final weight loss above 721 °C due to thermal decomposition of the polymer. Interestingly, the thermograms of copolymers CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3 indicate similar thermal profiles to each other, with the first weight loss of 19% observed in the temperature range of 178–354 °C attributed to the decomposition of Polypyrrole, Polyaniline and Poly p-Phenylenediamine chain from the copolymers [33,34], the second weight loss of 13% in the temperature range 463 °C due to oligomer decomposition, and the third weight loss above 623 °C due to decomposition of polymer.

Further confirmation of the successful polymer synthesis was obtained through XRD analysis, with the XRD spectra of POBBA and copolymers CP-1, CP-2, and CP-3 shown in Fig. 4 . The broad diffraction peaks of POBBA and copolymers are nearly identical in shape. Each includes a broad peak around 2θ = 13.4–26.9°, which can be attributed to the repeated units of pyrrole, aniline, and p-phenylenediamine in the copolymers [33]. The sharply diffracted peak of POBBA around 20.2°, which is not seen in the copolymer XRD spectra, can be due to the periodicity parallel to the polymer chains [33]. These extremely broad diffracted peaks indicate that the POBBA and its associated copolymers are amorphous in nature, as highly crystalline polymers would be expected to give much sharper peaks.

Morphology studies of the solid-state polymer and copolymer products were conducted using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), with SEM images of POBBA and the three copolymers shown in Fig. 5 . The SEM image of POBBA displayed agglomerated particles with irregular spherical shapes and a broad range of diameters between 200 and 900 nm. CP-1 forms similarly inhomogeneous particles, with a broad range diameter between 800 and 1500 nm and an approximately spherical shape. CP-2 forms irregular spherical shape particles around 500–1000 nm in size; and CP-3 formed cluster-like structures around 1000–2000 nm size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size measurement by dynamic light scattering (DLS)
ce:section.ce:para: The particle sizes of the polymer and copolymer products were determined by dynamic light scattering analyses conducted in aqueous dispersions. This analysis enabled a calculation of the average particle size and the polydispersity indices, with the results summarized in Table 3 and Fig. 6 . The DLS spectrum of the POBBA polymer solution displays two peaks, which correspond to two different populations of particles. 88% of the particles have nanosized diameters between 100 and 900 nm, and 12% of the particles have micron-sized diameters around 4957 nm. In the case of CP-1, the particle sizes ranged from 600 nm diameters to 2010 nm in diameter, whereas CP-2 had a size range of 597 nm in diameter and CP-3 showed a bimodal size distribution with ranges of 298 nm and 1053 nm for the smaller and larger sized particles, respectively. The polydispersity of the polymer and copolymers are displayed in Table 3, with the dispersity indices below 1.0 proving the particle nature of the polymers and strongly suggesting good processability of polymer and copolymer dispersions [31].

The polymeric products were also characterized using solid-state 13C NMR spectroscopy, with the resulting spectra shown in Fig. 7 . The solid-state NMR spectra of OBBA (monomer) (Fig. 7a) shows a peak “a” at 153.3 ppm corresponding to carbon singly bound to oxygen, a peak “b” at 144.1 ppm assigned to carbon singly bound to nitrogen, and peaks at 123.5 and 117.7 ppm attributed to aromatic carbons. In the case of POBBA, by contrast (Fig. 7a), broad peaks between 110 and 158 ppm are observed, with the complete disappearance of the sharper monomer peaks. The broad peaks are caused by large molecules with slow tumbling times relative to the time scale of NMR relaxation.

The spectra of copolymers CP-1, CP-2 and CP-3 are shown in Fig. 7b. The peaks “a” and “b” appear at 153 and 147 ppm in all copolymers and correspond to the carbon atoms in the 4,4′-diaminodiphenylether sub-unit. In the case of CP-1, which contains alternating pyrrole subunits, the peak “c” at 136 ppm corresponds to the carbon which is a part of CN bond between the OBBA subunit and the pyrrole ring. Peaks “d” and “g,” at 123.5 and 106.3 ppm, are due to the aromatic pyrrole carbons. Finally, peaks “e” and “f” observed at 121.1 and 118.3 ppm are attributed to the aromatic carbons of OBBA in copolymer unit and indicate success in achieving a copolymer structure. In the case of copolymer CP-2, peak “c” appears at 129.2 ppm and can be assigned to the carbon that is a part of CN bond between aromatic carbon and amine in OBBA. Peaks “d” and “f” observed at 118.3 and 116.4 ppm are due to the aromatic carbons of aniline ring; peak “e” appears at 117.1 ppm and is attributed to the unsubstituted aromatic carbon on the OBBA subunit. In the case of copolymer CP-3, the peak “d” appears at 131.2 ppm due to the carbon where the CN bond between the ortho-carbons of p-PDA conjugated with OBBA. The peak “f” observed at 121.2 is due to the non-substituted aromatic carbon of p-PDA and the peak “c” appears at 133.6 ppm can be attributed to the amine substituted aromatic carbon. The peak “e” at 123.6 ppm is assigned to the non-substituted aromatic carbons. Overall, these solid-state 13C NMR spectra confirm the formation of polymeric products.

The kinetic studies of polymerization were investigated, and we optimized the polymer yield as a function of reaction time, reaction temperature and initiator (i.e. C-dot) concentration. The temperature of the reaction plays a major role in influencing the reaction yield (Fig. 8 a), due to the necessity of elevated temperatures in initiating C-dot-based reaction acceleration. Overall, the conversion of monomer to polymer yield increased with increasing reaction temperature, with the highest percentage of the polymer formed from a 90 °C reaction temperature.

The polymer conversion is also depended on the concentration of the initiator, with the dependence summarized in Fig. 8b. This figure shows that the yield of polymer remains unchanged above 9 mg of C-dot initiator, indicating that even small amounts of initiator are sufficient to achieve high yielding polymerization. The data in Fig. 8c summarizes our efforts to optimize the reaction time, with ideal results (as measured by polymer yield) obtained after a 24-hour reaction time. A summary of the kinetic data is provided in Table 4 .

In summary, the present work reports a new mechanism for the polymerization of a diamine-functionalized, aryl ether OBBA monomer and three OBBA-containing copolymers using C-dots to promote the desired reactivity. The novelty of this work compared to previously published work from our group and others include the formation of new polymers with high thermal stability and spontaneous particle generation, the elimination of the requirement for UV-light as a co-initiator for the C-dot reaction promoters, and the relatively rapid polymerization reaction. Detailed characterization and kinetic studies confirm the formation of the target polymers and the mechanism by which such polymerization occurs. Future work will focus on applications of the newly synthesized polymer and copolymers for the adsorption of gases such as carbon dioxide and nitrogen, and the potential use of such adsorption in clean energy technology development.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Dr. Keren Keinan Adamsky and Dr. Michal Afri for assistance in carrying out the solid-state NMR analysis and Dr. Reut Cohen for assistance in carrying out TGA analysis in the chemistry department at Bar-Ilan University, Israel.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crit. Rev. Solid State Mater. Sci.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: L. Dai. Chapter 1 From conventional technology to carbon nanotechnology : the fourth industrial revolution and the discoveries of CQ, carbon nanotube and nanodiamond, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile one-step sonochemical synthesis of ultrafine and stable fluorescent C-dots
